metadata.title:
AUDIENCE DIVIDES ON


DIES MONIES PLAN

pages.raw_text:
in order to get the best men. I'm
der the present system of ward
councilmen men are elected to the
council not because of their ability
or honesty, but because they are
good vote getters.


Ex-Councilmen n. S. Pratt made
the chief talk for the negative. He
praised the present city charter
and said that many of the pro,
visions of the Des Moines plan,
particularly the initiative and ref
erendum, are Incorporated in 1t.
Such things as the charter now
lacks can be secured under. the
present system, without going to
the expense of changing the form
of government. Mr.. Pratt said he
favored enlarging the powers of
the mayor to great extent, giving
the mayor the right to fill all the
appointive offices under the char
ter and remove the men at will.


Mr.. Pratt saw elements of dan
ger in the Des Moines plan in that
it would give a large conservative
minority. which owned property
close in a chance to control the
city government, and thereby cut
out the man in the outlying dis
trict of a chance to have any i'm
provements made in his locality.
On this account he still held to the
system of electing ward council
men. He said the Des Moines plan
is called a business mems PLAN"
but he had little faith in business
men in running the city govern
ment, as they were in the past
nearly always lined up on the side
of the evil influences because it
was good for business.


Other speakers were c. c. Dill
of the high school and Attorney
Benson Wright for the Des Moines
idea, and M. w. Woodruff, mana
ger of the Barber Asphalt CO., and
William Matthews Of the Spokane
Tailoring Co., for the present plan.


Councilman c. w. Mohr of the
Fourth ward was on hand and put
in a good word for the existing
order of things, but said he was
ready to adopt any new or i'm
proved idea of government If
shown that it was an improve
ment.
